Motor Trend: Trasmission & MPG

Riccardo Del Signore

2025-08-31

  • 1 MOTOR TREND analysis
    • 1.1 Data & Methods
    • 1.2 Exploratorty Data Analysis (EDA)
    • 1.3 Models
    • 1.4 Diagnostic of residual
  • 2 Conclusions

1 MOTOR TREND analysis

1.1 Data & Methods

  • Dataset: mtcars
  • Outcome: mpg
  • Potential confounders: wt, hp , cyl, disp, gear, carb

1.2 Exploratorty Data Analysis (EDA)

  • Descriptive statistics
# summary stats of group per am

mt %>%
  group_by(am) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_mpg = mean(mpg),
    sd_mpg = sd(mpg),
    mean_wt = mean(wt),
    sd_wt = sd(wt),
    mean_hp = mean(hp),
    sd_hp = sd(hp),
    mean_cyl = mean(cyl),
    sd_cyl = sd(cyl)
  )
## # A tibble: 2 × 10
##   am            n mean_mpg sd_mpg mean_wt sd_wt mean_hp sd_hp mean_cyl sd_cyl
##   <fct>     <int>    <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>  <dbl>
## 1 Automatic    19     17.1   3.83    3.77 0.777    160.  53.9     6.95   1.54
## 2 Manual       13     24.4   6.17    2.41 0.617    127.  84.1     5.08   1.55
  • Boxplot MPG vs trasmissionn type
ggplot(mt, aes(x = am, y = mpg, fill = am)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "MPG by Transmission Type",
       x = "Transmission Type",
       y = "Miles Per Gallon (MPG)") +
  theme_minimal()

Figure 1 shows a clear separation between groups: manual cars tend to have higher MPG values and more variability compared with automatic cars.

  • Scatter Plot MPG vs wt
ggplot(mt, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, color = am, shape = am)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  labs(title = "MPG vs Weight",
       x = "Weight",
       y = "Miles Per Gallon (MPG)") +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Figure 2 suggests that the weight is strongly associated with fuel efficiency: heavier cars have a lower MPG. Manual cars are generally lighter, which may partly explain their higher fuel efficiency. This could suggest the need for adjusted models controlling weight as a confounder

1.3 Models

  • Model 1
lm_fit <- lm(mpg ~ am, data = mt)
summary(lm_fit, correlation = TRUE)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = mpg ~ am, data = mt)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -9.3923 -3.0923 -0.2974  3.2439  9.5077 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)   17.147      1.125  15.247 1.13e-15 ***
## amManual       7.245      1.764   4.106 0.000285 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 4.902 on 30 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.3598, Adjusted R-squared:  0.3385 
## F-statistic: 16.86 on 1 and 30 DF,  p-value: 0.000285
## 
## Correlation of Coefficients:
##          (Intercept)
## amManual -0.64
# coef and CI table

tidy(lm_fit, conf.int = TRUE)
## # A tibble: 2 × 7
##   term        estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high
##   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 (Intercept)    17.1       1.12     15.2  1.13e-15    14.9       19.4
## 2 amManual        7.24      1.76      4.11 2.85e- 4     3.64      10.8

amManual coefficient reveal that there is a mpg mean difference between Manual and Automatic with a p.value < 0.05

  • Model 2 adjusted
lm_fit2 <- lm(mpg ~ am + wt, data = mt)
tidy(lm_fit2, conf.int = TRUE)
## # A tibble: 3 × 7
##   term        estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high
##   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 (Intercept)  37.3        3.05    12.2    5.84e-13    31.1      43.6 
## 2 amManual     -0.0236     1.55    -0.0153 9.88e- 1    -3.18      3.14
## 3 wt           -5.35       0.788   -6.79   1.87e- 7    -6.96     -3.74

We can se the Simpson Paradox in action: the amManual coefficient changed from 7.24 to -1.12 when adjusting for wt, meaning that wt is a confounder.

lm_fit3 <- lm(mpg ~ am + wt + hp, data = mt)
tidy(lm_fit3, conf.int = TRUE)
## # A tibble: 4 × 7
##   term        estimate std.error statistic  p.value conf.low conf.high
##   <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 (Intercept)  34.0      2.64        12.9  2.82e-13  28.6      39.4   
## 2 amManual      2.08     1.38         1.51 1.41e- 1  -0.736     4.90  
## 3 wt           -2.88     0.905       -3.18 3.57e- 3  -4.73     -1.02  
## 4 hp           -0.0375   0.00961     -3.90 5.46e- 4  -0.0572   -0.0178

Comparing the model to evaluate the confounding effect of wt and hp on the am coefficient

anova(lm_fit, lm_fit2, lm_fit3)
## Analysis of Variance Table
## 
## Model 1: mpg ~ am
## Model 2: mpg ~ am + wt
## Model 3: mpg ~ am + wt + hp
##   Res.Df    RSS Df Sum of Sq      F    Pr(>F)    
## 1     30 720.90                                  
## 2     29 278.32  1    442.58 68.734 5.071e-09 ***
## 3     28 180.29  1     98.03 15.224 0.0005464 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

The p.value of the ANOVA test between model 1 and model 2 is <0.05, meaning that wt is a significant confounder. The p.value of the ANOVA test between model 2 and model 3 is < 0.05, meaning that hp is a significant confounder too but with a smaller effect on the am coefficient.

1.4 Diagnostic of residual

par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(lm_fit2, which = 1)
plot(lm_fit2, which = 2)
plot(lm_fit2, which = 3)
plot(lm_fit2, which = 4)

We examinated the residuals for the model includinng weight and trasmission.

  • 1 The residual vs fitted values plot showed no systematic patterns and the point are apparenntly ranndomly spearded around the 0, suggesting that linearity and homoscedasticity assumptions are met.
  • 2 The normal QQ-plot indicate approximate normality of residuals, which onnly deviate onn extremities.
  • 3 The Scale-location plot confirmed roughly constant variance across fitted values.
  • 4 Cook’s distance did not reveal influential outlier

Overall, diagnnostic check support the adequacy of the model.

2 Conclusions

Our analysis indicates that manual transmission cars are generally more fuel efficient than automatic cars. In unadjusted comparisons, manuals achieved on average ~7 MPG more than automatics. However, after adjusting for weight—a strong confounder—the estimated benefit of manual transmissions was reduced to about +2.9 MPG.

This suggests that while manual transmission itself is associated with improved fuel efficiency, part of the apparent advantage arises from the fact that manual cars in this dataset are lighter. Residual diagnostics supported the validity of the chosen model, and the results are consistent with the hypothesis that manual transmission provides a modest but real fuel economy benefit.
